author: @howarddavies3744
text: You were always providing interest and enthusiasm to the internet, you will be missed.

author: @TMGpuntNL
text:
i hate free will.

There are not many species on this planet that have a free will.

Most of the species on this planet behave like lemmings.
They just are alive and when it is time they die because of nature.

We human beings on the other hand have this ridiculous thing called: free will.

It does not help anyone.
The rich will use such an excuse for what they are doing while the poor will use the same excuse as to why they remain poor.

But if there was no such thing as free will, Robert would still be with us today.


So on the one side i am happy there is no way to control free will.
But on the other side, if it was controlled we would still have Robert making videos.

Robert: Rest in peace, my friend.
I will never understand,
why you thought this was the right way to end
all of what you have accomplished. 

Have a nice trip, compadre.

author: @adventureisoutthere8064
text:
I will miss Robert, he was always tinkering on the same concepts as myself.  Was hoping to get to meet up with him on a trip to England.

If this Parts list helps others (US Based):
solar panel  disconnect switch: (44$USD)
> Solar Panels Disconnect Switch 32Amp AC Solar Shut Off Switch 400V 2 Poles, IP65 Waterproof UV-Proof for Solar PV Homes
> Solar Panels Disconnect Switch 16A 1000VDC PV Shut Off Switch Outdoor Circuit (over kill per video  2:20)

solar battery isolator switch: (13$USD)
> Kohree Battery Disconnect Switch, 12V-48V Battery Power Cut Master Switch for Car, RV ,Boat (on/off)  (video 3:30)

Solar Power Charge Controller (90$USD)  (Video 4:37)
> (Ships from Germany) ECO-WORTHY 30A Charge Controller Solar 12V/24V, Solar Charge Controller Intelligent Controller with Two USB Ports Solar Battery Controller LCD Display


DC Fuse Distribution Block (32-64$USE)  (Video: 5:16)
>Blue Sea Systems 5026 ST Blade Fuse Block 12 Circuit with Ground and Cover, 100 Amps

In a time where it’s needed the most. Educating the common people on what should be the essentials. Fly high soldier 🕊️ we love you and may your work live on in the seeds of the future tinkeres ❤

author: @Kanuka-Forest
text: A good little system Rob, but your DC distribution fuse board should really come from the battery not the pwm load port. The DC load capacity in amps is quite low from those small controllers, you could blow it connecting too many things from it.

author: @jonaslennstrom2752
text:
Good you mention batteries need to be connected to charge controller first, and then solar panels.
Vice versa when disconnecting, disconnect solar panels first.
This is very important as the charge controller may die if you have solar connected but no battery.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: May I suggest that using a 150 watt inverter for a load of 60 watt is running in a bit close. Remember 60 watt load means 90 watt input to the inverter as there is no such thing as a 100% efficient inverter. And if the load happens to start up at double it's normal 60 Watts which means you're looking at 120 Watts startup, on the output which then it means you're looking at around 180 watt on the input. You may find that the inverter will trip due to overload.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thanks for the heads up - much appreciated!

author: @GerbenWulff
text: I recommend you put another switch between the two batteries and wire the batteries in parallel. That way during normal operation, the batteries will be in parallel and have the same voltage. Then when you want to do a test, you simply disconnect the batteries from each other and you have both batteries at the same voltage, which makes a better comparison if you do a test. Also the batteries will degrade at the same rate if you use them in parallel.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @q3y6
text: I have a similar split set up in my shed/cabin but I've gone 12v DC (lead acid batteries) and 240 AC (Lithium battery). The big difference though is I take the load from the battery rather than the charge controller, charge controllers have a limited capacity, much better and safer to go direct from the battery.

author: @offgridwanabe
text: Can you explain why you used the load terminal on the charge controller instead of the battery directly seems to me it will restrict your amperage which maybe ok for the lights but maybe not for the printer

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Interestingly a lot of folks have suggested running straight from the battery and in lots of applications that would be fine. But running straight from the battery means you are using an unregulated supply and a battery will vary the voltage between anything like 10-14 volts - sometimes even more. That can be damaging in some applications. The Controller output is regulated. Of course you could put in a voltage regulator or apply regulation at the point of use.

replies.author: @offgridwanabe
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thanks I had not thought it needed regulating but my guess is it will stop any flickering Cheers.

author: @davewardvodafone
text: Sorry but you can only draw a very limited current from the load side of a controller all your loads need to be taken from the switched side of the battery (I highly recommend a busbar at this point to supply your loads ) or you will damage your controller in a very short time due to overheating as it’s only rated for a very small current.

replies.author: @urbanimage
replies.text: A puzzling choice, taking the DC load from the controller rather than the batteries.

replies.author: @loup754
replies.text: Not puzzling at all. Controller will prevent battery discharging beyond low limit and damaging battery.

replies.author: @urbanimage
replies.text: @loup754 It may make some sense if all you plan to connect is something that doesn't draw a lot of power like the fairy lights shown in the video, and where you could also make use of the timer function these controllers tend to have on the dc load to switch them on and off, but other than that only coming off the controller is rather limiting.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Yes it is - but i will only be using a very small current - and the current supply from the controller is regulated - that's not so for the battery - of course you could add a regulator if you want - it's like I said in the video mate - everyone makes a choice and you go with what is good for you - If you want to draw from the battery - go for it - good choice.

replies.author: @davewardvodafone
replies.text:
Yes but you imply that you could run an inverter from it and you could not! Because of the current needed. And to be sure you don’t take the battery’s below 50% where they would be damaged you will need an indicator for % left and deal with it manually or a voltage cut off to prevent any damage.

And this is where a choice of battery comes into play as most lithium battery’s have a BMS battery monitoring system which does all of this for you!
